snippet.title: CEO Bernard Looney on bp's Strategic Transformation - CSC Global Leadership Series
snippet.description:
Bernard Looney CEO of bp discusses the company's strategic transformation and net zero by 2050 aspirations.

CSC Global Alumni in partnership with CSC UK hosted the launch of the Global Leadership Series in the United Kingdom on the 8th of September 2021.

In the presence of Her Royal Highness The Princess Royal, the CEO of bp, Bernard Looney, discussed leadership and sustainability within the context of climate, communities, energy and the environment. The event was hosted at Coutts London by CEO and CSC UK Vice Chair,​ Peter Flavel​, and CSC UK Chair Sir Alan Parker CVO. 

CSC alumni from over 25 Commonwealth countries attended the event via video link.

snippet.title: Leaders with Lacqua: Bernard Looney, BP CEO
snippet.description:
Bernard Looney is a pandemic leader, taking over as CEO of one of the world's largest oil companies just weeks before Covid-19 shut down the global economy. It's also been a time of increasing pressure from activists and investors on the industry to decarbonize. Looney joins Bloomberg's Francine Lacqua for a wide-ranging conversation about his career, overhauling a company during the pandemic, and opportunities in the energy transition.

snippet.title: BP makes Looney profits as we all still endure the Cost of Living Crisis.
snippet.description:
BP has posted profits of £5bn on its oil and gas business, as we continue to face high petrol & heating bills.
So how are you enjoying your new ridiculously high energy bills then? No doubt you’ll be delighted to know BP – good old British Petroleum – have seen their profits double to £5bn as they rake it in off the back of high energy and petrol prices, or as they’ve put it ‘exceptional oil and gas trading’. British company folks, doubling its profits whilst we’re paying through the nose. 
It was only back in March that BP’s boss, Bernard Looney – no shit, that’s his name – was gloating about seeing his £1.3m annual salary get boosted to £4.5m in performance related bonuses, so at least you know where some of that extra you’re forking out is going. He’s also on record as saying prices won’t let up for some time. Well why would they? He’s raking it in, his company is raking it in and there’s no sign on the government intervening in the form of a windfall tax or demanding they take a hit to those massive profits as energy providers owned by other countries have been forced to do. According to the Tories it would put off investment by these companies. Well if those investments aren’t benefitting us, just costing us more, who exactly are these investments benefitting? It’s not even true as is so often the case, since BP are currently investing £18bn between now and 2030 in the UK. But people need help now and it needs providing meaningfully. Wholesale gas prices for example at the end of last month, were 41% lower than they were in December, yet our bills are now 54% higher. How is this fair?
This French Twitter user received an email from EDF, one of our big 6 providers, but is a French company, informing them that their electric bill is too high and that they’re reducing it, from E87 to E73 - £73 down to £61 in our money – God we can only dream of having electric bills that low! God, don’t you just wish we still owned our own energy infrastructure and the Tories didn’t flog it all off to become mainly foreign owned? localized.title: BP makes Looney profits as we all still endure the Cost of Living Crisis.
localized.description:
BP has posted profits of £5bn on its oil and gas business, as we continue to face high petrol & heating bills.
So how are you enjoying your new ridiculously high energy bills then? No doubt you’ll be delighted to know BP – good old British Petroleum – have seen their profits double to £5bn as they rake it in off the back of high energy and petrol prices, or as they’ve put it ‘exceptional oil and gas trading’. British company folks, doubling its profits whilst we’re paying through the nose. 
It was only back in March that BP’s boss, Bernard Looney – no shit, that’s his name – was gloating about seeing his £1.3m annual salary get boosted to £4.5m in performance related bonuses, so at least you know where some of that extra you’re forking out is going. He’s also on record as saying prices won’t let up for some time. Well why would they? He’s raking it in, his company is raking it in and there’s no sign on the government intervening in the form of a windfall tax or demanding they take a hit to those massive profits as energy providers owned by other countries have been forced to do. According to the Tories it would put off investment by these companies. Well if those investments aren’t benefitting us, just costing us more, who exactly are these investments benefitting? It’s not even true as is so often the case, since BP are currently investing £18bn between now and 2030 in the UK. But people need help now and it needs providing meaningfully. Wholesale gas prices for example at the end of last month, were 41% lower than they were in December, yet our bills are now 54% higher. How is this fair?

God, don’t you just wish we still owned our own energy infrastructure and the Tories didn’t flog it all off to become mainly foreign owned? We pay more, so other countries don’t have to, we subsidise their national bills and even as wholesale prices fall, our bills don’t.  But at least the Looney’s of the world are doing well.

snippet.title: Sir Alan Parker Closing Remarks - CSC Global Leadership Series London 2021
snippet.description:
CSC Global Alumni in partnership with CSC UK hosted the launch of the Global Leadership Series in the United Kingdom on the 8th of September 2021.

In the presence of Her Royal Highness The Princess Royal, the CEO of bp, Bernard Looney, discussed leadership and sustainability within the context of climate, communities, energy and the environment. The event was hosted at Coutts London by CEO and CSC UK Vice Chair,​ Peter Flavel​, and CSC UK Chair Sir Alan Parker CVO. 

CSC alumni from over 25 Commonwealth countries attended the event via video link.

snippet.title: S3:Ep13 - Why CEOs Should Care about Starting a Digital Conversation with Expert Tim Hughes
snippet.description:
The world of selling has irrevocably changed.

Buyers no longer want to be sold to and especially not as a result of a cold call. They want to get to know a company by getting to know and trust the people who work there. Starting digital conversations is the first step to build this trust. 

As CEO, it’s imperative that you lead by example. In this episode Alice talks with Tim Hughes who explains how to change your sales approach from “interrupting to pitch” to “gaining trust”. 

Simply put, you and your team use social media to start conversations that both expand your network and build trust for a future opportunity to sell. 

He explains the three how-to steps: Creating the right social profile, expanding your network, producing content that engages. 

Many salespeople don’t know how to use social media to connect and build a trusted network so they simply have a meager profile and don’t do anything else. 

In fact they may even be afraid because their previous company may have fired employees who posted on social. 

If you expect your sales or better yet, your Go To Market team to build networks and share content on social then as CEO model the behavior and create the proper framework for success including training, guidelines and content strategies.

Tim goes on to explain the opportunities for B2B CEOs to dominate their vertical as thought leaders by producing content designed to educate target customers. 

Unless you are one of a handful of CEOs like Bernard Looney of BP, who already have it down, it’s time to watch, learn and do social selling.

About Our Guest

Tim Hughes is universally recognized as the world leading pioneer and exponents of Social Selling and he is currently ranked Number 1 by Onalytica as the most influential social selling person in the world. In 2021, LinkedIn said he was one of the top 8 sales experts globally to follow. 

He is also Co-Founder and CEO of DLA Ignite and co-author of the bestselling books “Social Selling - Techniques to Influence Buyers and Changemakers” and “Smarketing - How To Achieve Competitive Advantage through blended Sales and Marketing”.  Both published by Kogan Page.


Social Selling: Techniques to Influence Buyers and Changemakers
https://www.amazon.com/Social-Selling-Techniques-Influence-Changemakers-dp-1398695319/dp/1398695319/ref=mt_other?_encoding=UTF8&me=&qid=1665839673

snippet.title: Interview with Coutts' CEO Peter Flavel - Inspirational element of working with companies that care
snippet.description:
Coutts' CEO Peter Flavel shares the firm's work on governance and business impact assessments

This interview is part of ESG Calrity's Best B Haviour video series focusing on a coalition of B Corp certified businesses - those companies that encourage better governance within financial services.

Last year, Bonhill titles ESG Clarity, DiversityQ, Portfolio Adviser, International Adviser and Expert Investor responded to industry events surrounding treatment of customers and stakeholders with the Campaign for Better Governance, and meanwhile at COP26, a coalition of B Corps companies came together to issue a call to action to financial services to implement tighter and deeper corporate governance frameworks. As a result of this shared ambition, Best B Haviour was formed.

Over the next few weeks, ESG Clarity will interview the CEOs and senior management of B Corps companies discussing the importance of the campaigns, shareholder versus stakeholder centrism and internal governance.

In this episode, ESG Clarity’s global head of ESG insight Natalie Kenway speaks to Peter Flavel, CEO at Coutts.

He discusses how assisting a client firm in becoming B Corp Certified led to Coutts being inspired to signing up. He explains how business impact assessments (BIAs) and changing articles of association can assist firms.

He tells listeners about the “inspirational element of working with companies that care and wanting to do the right thing by all staff” and why it is important to look at companies in this way to retain young talent.

He also talks about Coutts’ governance structure and the work it is doing across the five areas of the the BIA, and why it is spending more time focusing on supply chains who, he hopes, will join them on the B Corps journey.

See also: – Coutts advances ESG efforts - https://esgclarity.com/coutts-advances-esg-efforts/

snippet.title: Twilio's Jeff Lawson on Building Software with Superpowers
snippet.description:
When it comes to innovation, Twilio CEO and co-founder Jeff Lawson has simple advice: "Build or die." Lawson straddles the line of software developer and CEO, giving him a rare perspective on the common ways the two sides misunderstand each other. It also gave him an early, deep understanding of developers’ needs in a software platform — which has been borne out in Twilio’s success. Lawson joined Greylock general partner Sarah Guo for Iconversations to discuss business trajectory, and what he sees happening in the future of software and the internet.

snippet.title: Opportunity and Sustainability - CSC Global Leadership Series
snippet.description:
CSC Global Alumni in partnership with CSC UK hosted a CSC Global Leadership Series Event on the 10th of March 2022. The topic "Banking on a green economy - the role of financial services in achieving sustainability" discusses what drives leaders and organisations to commit to being more environmentally sustainable and why the opportunities for investment in sustainability and climate change are an important catalyst for businesses and governments to make commitments to net zero. 

In this video the panel discuss how the green transition to achieve 2030 and 2050 climate and net zero targets are a massive business opportunity and why all businesses should be considering the implications and opportunities to invest in the green economy and making their own organisations more sustainable.. The panel addresses how banks can play a role in ensuring net zero is kept in focus in the face of the current Ukraine crisis and the consequences of inaction.

The panel for the discussion included:

Sir Alan Parker - Chairman of Brunswick Group
Peter Flavel - CEO Coutts Bank
Bill Winters - CEO of Standard Chartered
Jennifer Jordan-Saifi - Assistant Private Secretary of The Prince of Wales' Sustainable Markets Initiative.
